top_profane.text:
I haven't actually bought this item, yet.

But I was THINKING about buying it, today, which is kind of an example of the law of unintended consequences. I will explain:

I park my car outside, because up until recently, one-half of our garage was filled with junk. I didn't INTEND it to be filled with junk, it just ended up that way, because a few years back, our city changed the way garbage pickup worked. It used to be that you could put pretty much any old thing out at the curb for the trash pickup. You'd have your garbage cans, sure, but if you, say, broke a kitchen chair you could put that out there and they'd just pick it up and take it. Couch cushions, old drawers, whatever it was that broke around the house, you could throw away, even if it didn't fit in the can.

That was because back then, garbage men got out of the truck and picked up the can and dumped it in the truck, so they could also pick up your old chair or floor lamp or novelty statue, too. So that worked great, except for the time we had a tree branch fall on our plastic garbage can and break it, so that the garbage can was dented permanently and cracked and no good for its intended purpose of holding garbage.

The week after that happened, I took the can out to the curb with the full garbage cans, and left them all there. When I came home from work, the broken, dented can was still there, next to its empty, still-functional compatriots.

So the next week, I put a note on the garbage can that said "I AM GARBAGE PLEASE TAKE ME," and that worked.

Anyway, after they switched the system so that we had to use special garbage cans that could be picked up by a mechanical arm, so that the garbage man didn't need to get out of his truck, at all, we couldn't throw out just anything anymore: it had to fit in the can, so that ratty armchair from the basement, the toy basketball hoop, the old television: all had to be specially marked and hauled away, at a cost of $25 per item, if not more.

I am philosophically opposed to paying $25 to throw something away, and so I just didn't. When the first thing broke -- I think it was the old snowblower my dad had unloaded on us one time, a snowblower that was only 6" wide and had a tiny electric motor, so that it would only blow the absolute finest, powdery, wispy snow -- snow that was the snow equivalent of Tinkerbell's fairy dust -- and even if you needed to blow, as opposed to shovel or simply wish away that kind of snow, even then the blower would only do a 6" swath at a time, which is useless on a 14' wide driveway -- when that first thing broke, I put it in the garage and thought...

...well, nothing. I didn't have a plan, other than "I'm not paying to throw it away, so here it sits." I suppose in the back of my mind I may have intended someday to petition the government to redress my grievance vis a vis the trash, and reinstate trash pickup the way God intended it, and then I'd haul the snowblower to the curb and let it be taken away. I suppose, too, that maybe I just assumed, contrary to all experience and reason, that nothing bigger than a garbage can would ever break in our house again, but in each case, my (possible?) assumption was wrong, and things continued to break, and those things continued to be too big to put in a garbage can. An old entertainment center I'd bought in college that had made its way from our living room to the den to the kids' bedrooms finally gave up the ghost and went to the garage. We had an old garbage can from the pre-modernized days, and we couldn't throw away the old garbage cans, because they didn't fit in the new one. I had a second lawnmower that didn't work, and I don't even know where that came from! Maybe my dad, maybe junk just multiplied, I don't know.

A television set. Another one -- we go through TVs quickly, it seemed-- then a third and a fourth. A broken yard rake. The old double stroller that the boys grew out of and which we deemed too gross to give to Goodwill. The old window air conditioner. A cement block that probably was left there by the prior owner of our house.

Eventually, this stuff crowded my car out of my side of the garage, and I parked on the driveway. I live in Wisconsin, a state whose motto ought to be "Nobody Actually Wants To Live Here," because Wisconsin suffers from 14 months per year of winter, which sounds impossible but isn't: we have extra winter, so much extra winter that we layer it on, so January in Wisconsin isn't just "January," it's like "Triple January," and things ice up and freeze over and get windblown and buried under snowfall and otherwise blind you with their sheer winterness, and that's why one day, with my car parked outside, I walked outside to start it and my car was covered in sheet ice.

Have you ever seen a car covered in sheet ice? It is BREATHTAKING, unless you have to get to work and are already running late because Mr F, your second-youngest son, wanted you to tickle him for a while before you went to work.

I chipped away at the ice to open my door and then got in the car, and I'm no dummy: I started it up and let it run for the required fourteen seconds that I figure the car needs to warm up, and then I blasted the vents that blow on the windshield to start melting the ice on the windshield, because here's the thing: I don't have an ice scraper.

Don't ask me why. The answer would simply be "because I don't have one," and I don't have a good reason why other than the only time you think to buy an ice scraper, at least if you're me, is when you need one, and you only ever need an ice scraper on a Tuesday morning when you are running late and your car is covered in sheet ice, which is exactly when you CANNOT go get one, so you get into your car and you start it and you turn the defroster on but it's not going fast enough, and you don't want to try driving to work when you can't see out of the windshield at ALL, so you've got to help it along and generate some heat and what generates heat?

FRICTION! YES! Go to the head of the class. So you start up the windshield wipers and watch in amazement as the arm of the windshield wiper in front of the driver pulls and pulls but the wiper is iced to the windshield and finally the arm BREAKS OFF and the wiper stays iced to the bottom of the windshield with the tiny robot arm -- robot arms are behind ALL of my problems, practically -- and you're STILL no closer to getting to leave for work so you grab the one thing that you have in the car which might CONCEIVABLY scrape ice: an empty "Faygo Red Pop" soda can, which you use to try to scrape the ice in front of the driver, but all that does is scratch the windshield RIGHT AT EYE LEVEL, and finally you are forced to admit defeat, use your OWN BARE HANDS to get rid of some of the ice, and then drive to work peering through a tiny sliver of space on the windshield.

"But," some of you are saying, "That was January! Why are you looking at windshield wiper blades here in JULY?"

The answer, you perceptive few, is this: You only think of windshield wiper blades when you NEED them, which is to say: when you are driving and it begins to rain, and you turn on the windshield wipers, and the wiper arm, which you have sort of reattached but not really, begins to splay wildly around the window, not wiping the water at all but rather doing a bizarre, crablike dance, right before your eyes...

...I should note that the PASSENGER SIDE windshield wiper, the side where NOBODY EVER SITS and which is USELESS TO ME AS A DRIVER, works just FINE. That side of the windshield is immaculate...

...and you'd stop and replace the wiper blade, but you have to GET SOMEWHERE, which is why you are driving in the FIRST PLACE, and so you cannot stop to get a windshield wiper blade now, you've got THINGS TO DO, and by the time you CAN stop to get a windshield wiper blade, you no longer NEED to do so.

And so it has been nearly six months that I have been without a windshield wiper blade, a fact I attribute 100% to my local municipalities' decision to change the way garbage pickup worked 7 or 8 years ago.

I should note that I did not, today, set out to buy a windshield wiper blade. Instead, I took a break at work to see what I might buy on Amazon using a gift card I had, and I hit the clearance/sale tab, and saw that they had windshield wiper blades on sale, and so I clicked that link and found that there is a bewildering array of windshield wiper sizes and styles and the like, and so rather that work my way through them to actually BUY one, I have opted to write this review, instead.

I give this product five stars.

EPILOGUE:

The junk in my garage is gone. I rented a truck and took it to the garbage dump myself, two weeks ago. It cost me $175 total, by the time I was done. I only did it because my wife, Sweetie, insisted that we do that. On the downside, I am now out $175. On the upside, I could park my car in that side of the garage, except that somewhere along the line the garage door opener broke and I'm far too lazy to actually get out of my car, open the door, get back in and pull the car in, then go close the door. So I still park on the driveway. That's not the REAL upside. The REAL upside is that I learned that there are actual buzzards that live at the dump.

top_profane.text:
This black garbage can comes with letters 'V-Y-B' stamped in gold on the front. It measures 17 inches by 13 inches by 2 inches making it the ideal size for any vehicle. The receptacle itself is quite lightweight at a mere 14.4 ounces when it's empty. This trash can is the ideal way for you to stash your trash while on the go in your vehicle so it stays clean. It's designed to install, remove, empty and clean quite easily. When it's time to get rid of the trash, you won't have to clean your entire car. All you have to do is empty the bag, white it out and it's ready to go for another round. This particular garbage receptacle features a spring frame opening that stay closed when it's not in use to keep your trash out of site. It also comes with some disposable garbage bags (pictured) that you can use to line your new garbage can to make clean up even easier.

I'll be honest, when I first found out I would be writing this review entry, I didn't know which direction I wanted to take to make this entry more informational. I thought about talking about the effects of littering so that you would be more inclined to put trash in its proper place. However, I changed my mind because I found some thing better. I found an article I really want to share with you that was published by Jalopnik in July 2012. The article is titled Ten Ways to Keep Your Car Clean Even if You're Lazy.

DON'T EAT IN THERE

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: If you can avoid eating in your car, you won't have to deal with all the wrappers, cartons, stains and crumbs that inevitably follow.

WAX IT OCCASIONALLY

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: It means you have to buy something and it means you have to spend some time after washing your car, but it's an investment that pays off. Waxing your car occasionally makes washing it the next time easier.

KNOCK YOUR SHOES BEFORE GETTING IN

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: Wherever it snows and gets slushy, you really can't keep your car from getting dirty on the inside, but you can do a lot just by knocking your boots before you get in.

Sit down on the seat with your legs outside and knock your cold, block-like feet together. Then you just swivel in. It means your footwells won't be quite so grimy and icy.

CLEAN MESSES UP QUICK

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: If you let a spill or a stain linger for any period of time in your car, it quickly becomes "character" and is impossible to remove.

Remember that time when Jim smacked his head on the doorframe and got blood on the armrest? Of course you do, because you didn't clean it up right away and now it's a stain you never have the time or the wherewithal to scrub away.

GARAGE IT

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: While not everyone has a garage available, if you can find some enclosed space for your car, it will stay much cleaner. Mother Nature has a way of bringing every living bird to roost in the tree just above your parked car.

There are a thousand other things the elements can bring unto your vehicle, so clear out your old BowFlex and stick your car in there.

KEEP A TRASH BAG IN THE CAR

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: Get either a small trash can or just a trash bag and keep it in your car. It keeps your junk from falling into the tiny cracks around the seats, or from rolling under the seats, and generally getting where you're too lazy to clean.

EVERY TIME YOU LEAVE, TAKE SOMETHING OUT

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: There are three cleaning regimens that will keep your car clean. Each one requires a different degree of diligence, but they all work.

Every time you stop for gas, reach into all the cubby holes and pull out the trash so you can throw it out.

Every time you stop and get out of your car for any reason, grab all the trash in your car.

If you stop and your car already looks clean, look around for one thing in your car that doesn't strictly need to be there and take it out.

MAKE A ROUTINE FOR WASHING

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: There's no quick fix to keeping your car washed regularly. You have to either set up a schedule for taking your car to a carwash, or you need to start a routine for washing your own car.

Mark it out on your calendar and use the two-bucket method. One bucket has clean water and the other bucket is soapy. Scrub your car clean, soap it up, then wash it all off from the top down. Stay regimented and your car won't ever stay dirty.

KEEP IT CLEAN SO IT STAYS CLEAN

WHY IT'S EFFECTIVE: The easiest way to keep your car from getting dirty is to clean it once and make sure it stays that way. Once it's clean, it's easy to keep it clean, as reader one reader explains

If your car is always clean, you will be more inclined to toss out any trash right away. Similar to the dirty laundry analogy: If you have one pair of dirty socks on the floor, it's much easier to put that second pair there, then the third, then the.... Eventually it becomes a mess.

Also, if your passengers see a clean car, they will notice and clean up after themselves, not to mention compliment your ride.

If your family is anything like mine, no matter how hard you try when you get a new or new-to-you vehicle, it seems like the backseat resembles a landfill at some point. I absolutely hate cleaning my car (even more than I hate cleaning the house) so I definitely fall into the 'lazy' category. Even though I'm lazy when it comes to cleaning my car, I fund the article above had some very helpful tips that are easy to implement and turn from a challenge into a habit to make car cleaning even easier in the future.

The great thing about the garbage can featured in this review entry is that it's compact enough to store either in the front or back on the floor, yet it's large enough to hold larger ticket items such as burger containers, coffee cups and even dirty diapers without spilling over the sides. I also like how it comes with a 100% lifetime guarantee. That tells me this company really stands behind the quality of their product.

top_profane.text:
I don’t know who’s car this trash can was designed for, but it definitely needs a design rework. The square shape is way too impractical for use in the cabin; As you can see from my picture, the trash can takes up HALF the thigh room in the front passenger seat. And not only that, my glove box is unable to be fully opened because the trash can is in the way; And yes, this is with the passenger seat scooted ALL THE WAY back.

Aside from the impractical shape, the Velcro used is extremely cheap. When I try to secure the flap to the Velcro, I can easily dislodge it with just a light pull from my pinky finger. I don’t understand how such a faulty Velcro connection system could pass QC for a trash can designed to be in a MOVING vehicle; Last thing I want in my car is to see my trash can potentially expel its contents in my footwell.

Besides my complaints, the trash can at least hold a good amount of trash; It will easily swallow all kinds of cans, bottles, and fast food baggies. The silicone cover at the top feels sturdy and will help keep the trash in(albeit the flimsy Velcro lid I mentioned earlier).

I think this trash can would’ve been so much better if it was a more conventional rectangle shape to save on space; It just simply takes up too much room being a square shape. The only place I can see this trash can working well is ONLY in the trunk space, where you don’t need to worry about leg room, passenger space, and/or less concerned about trash flying out of the lid. But considering this is advertised as a trash can that can be used inside your car cabin, I will review it as such.

I wanted to give it 3 Stars because it still does its job of swallowing trash pretty well, but the weak Velcro system really makes it hard to trust it in keeping the trash contents secure. Overall, a 2-Star product because of the lame Velcro, as well as all the above issues.

For reference, I have a Nissan Versa so my car isn’t very big, but definitely a fairly representative example of sub-compact car drivers.
top_profane.title: Can hold plenty of trash; Square shape is very IMPRACTICAL

top_profane.text:
Everyone who drives an automobile needs a car trash bag. My previous car trash bag purchase on Amazon - the High Road TrashStash Leakproof Hanging Car Trash Bag with Spring Closure @ $13.99 - cannot be recommended because the unit came apart at the glued seams within a year of moderate use. Since the product was otherwise satisfactory, I crossed my fingers and purchased a second unit - which promptly failed in the same fashion. It was time to try something new.

The House Day Car Trash Can with Lid seemed like a great choice. This product arrived promptly and was satisfactorily packaged. The trash can is basically a sturdy, soft-sided, waterproof, lidded & rectangular receptacle with straps. It is surprisingly handsome and upscale-looking. Here's what I liked about it upon receiving it from Amazon.

PROS:
* Seemingly sturdy overall construction
* High quality, black nylon fabric
* Adjustable straps which stay in place
* Waterproof plastic inner-liner
* Stiff, cardboard-reinforced Velcro-closure lid
* Flexible plastic, circular quick-toss trash insert-iris
* Multiple side pockets and plastic hooks for hanging things

After examining the item for obvious defects, I mounted it behind my vehicle passenger seat from the headrest posts. I was pleased to see the adjustable strap secured the trash can properly without any sagging. Unfortunately, after a few weeks of moderate use, I discovered a few flaws.

CONS:
* Only a small amount of trash can be stored in the unit
* The lid covers the top but not the sides of the bag - the stiff lid does not grip the sides of the trash can - leaving moderate gaps
* The Flexible plastic, circular quick-toss trash insert-iris doesn't really shut - it generally stays open

My primary gripe with the trash bag is the fact that the lid - when closed - allows the trash to be seen once the bag itself ceases to conform to the lid's rectangular shape. I will likely sew cardboard-reinforced, black fabric side-flaps to the lid which overhang the bag's sides and cover the gaps between the lid and the bag. Since I paid money for this new item, I shouldn't have to alter it in any way - but such is life. My secondary gripe is that the quick-toss iris mostly remains open, which to some degree defeats the purpose of a lid.

Should you buy this item? If you don't place smelly trash in the bag in the summer, which will attract insects into your vehicle, you might like the House Day Car Trash Can. If you don't like seeing what's in the bag every time you open your car door, you may want to look elsewhere.

Rating: 3 Out Of 5 Stars.

- Chipper F. Xavier, Esq.
top_profane.title: House Day Car Trash Can with Lid... A Mixed Bag.

top_rant.text:
LOVE LOVE LOVE the way these ACANII clear turn/marker lenses look in/on my truck!!!! Bought them to match my new SPYDER halo projector lenses and the new fog light lenses I installed and man does the front end of my truck look good and POP now!!!! All of my old headlight, turn signal/marker, and fog light lenses were all fogged up REALLY BADLY and just looked ABSOLUTELY HORRIBLE, my old foglight lenses were even filled over halfway up with water when I took them off, IDK how the old halogen bulbs in them didn't explode or catch fire!!!! You could barely see the road in front of you while driving at night especially on a back country road with no streetlights or anything else around, it was really bad!!!! I installed all of the following bulbs into these ACANII clear turn/marker lenses: the 4114K 30SMD deep blue LED daytime running light bulbs, the 3517 30SMD deep blue LED turn signal bulbs, and the deep blue 194 LED marker/turn signal bulbs and man am I happy with how they look!!!! I installed these ACANII clear lenses and all the mentioned LED bulbs to match the 12000k FANATELLI deep blue LED fog light bulbs I installed into my new fog light lenses and the 12000k HID low and high beam kit's I installed into my new SPYDER projector lenses!!!! Man oh man does it look so so good and mean as hell too, especially at night time with the entire front-end of my truck throwing out deep blue light from every single bulb through these ACANII clear lenses, the new clear fog light lenses, and the clear SPYDER halo projector lenses, I LOVE IT SO FREAKIN MUCH, IT IS SO FREAKIN BRIGHT NOW!!!! But I also know people in front of me and also people coming towards me ABSOLUTELY HATE IT SO MUCH because it is definitely bright when I'm behind you/coming towards you to say the least!!!! At night time if I'm behind or coming towards you, almost every car either way flashes their brights at me every single night, I think it's funny as hell and I ABSOLUTELY LOVE THAT TOO😈!!!! I definitely have no problems whatsoever seeing when I'm driving at night time anymore, I never even have to use my brights/high beams anymore, IDK why I even bought the HID bright/high beam kit's cause I NEVER have to use them anymore!!!! One thing you do need to be aware of when or if you decide to get these ACANII clear lenses and replace your regular old halogen turn signals with any LED bulbs (no matter the color) is that when you install the LED bulbs in the turn signal spot they use way less power than the regular old halogen bulbs do/did so your vehicle thinks that it has a burnt out bulb instead of a bulb that's just using less power (the vehicle doesn't know and can't tell the difference)!!!! So it is going to act like it has a burnt out bulb and it's going to make your turn signals do the super fast flashing deal when you use them (just like when you would have a burnt out halogen turn signal bulb how your turn signal would flash really fast basically to let you know that you have a burnt out bulb) which is really annoying and on top of that in newer model vehicles it's also going to either turn on your check engine light or your check/faulty bulb light as well!!!! So to fix this issue you are going to have to install resistors on/with any turn signal bulbs you change out to LED bulbs!!!! You can buy just the resistors by themselves and splice them into your factory wiring harness (which I do not recommend) or do like I did and pay a little bit more and get a resistor wiring harness kit that comes with the resistors already installed in them and all you have to do is plug one end of the harness into the factory plug where you would regularly plug in the bulb and the other end has a factory looking connector where you plug the LED bulb into and it fits right back into the turn signal lens housings!!!! Also just make sure you mount the resistors to SOMETHING METAL with METAL SCREWS (you can go to Lowe's or any hardware store and buy a pack of self-tapping metal screws that will go through just about anything for only like 5-8 bucks!) ABSOLUTELY NOTHING PLASTIC (no matter how sturdy or strong it looks/feels!) and away from any other wires because when the resistors are running (they are running whenever your lights are on) they get super hot, so if their is a wire touching a resistor it's going to melt right through that wire and your going to have a short circuit, a blown fuse, or potentially even a fire, which is really really bad for your vehicle if you didn't already know that!!!! Same thing goes for mounting them using zipties (we all know how great zipties can be but not with these bad boy's!) to mount them to anything either because like I said they will melt anything plastic and zipties are plastic so what do you think will happen if you use zipties to mount them?? Right kid's, they will melt right through them and they will fall down/off of whatever you ziptied them to!!!! But I would definitely VERY HIGHLY RECOMMEND using the pre-made wiring harnesses I'm talking about like the one's I bought from MANKK 1 and you can buy them right here on Amazon from several other different seller's too if you'd like!!!! Like I said I got mine from a seller called Mankk 1, they came as a pair, and they were only like 15 or 20 bucks for the pair!!!! They were very well made with good quality wiring, plugs, and insulation, and installed very easily!!!! Whomever you decide to buy them from just make sure you check the reviews out on them 1st before you buy them!!!! Anyways back to these ACANII clear turn/marker lenses, these lenses are AWESOME AND I WOULD HIGHLY RECOMMEND THEM TO ANYONE JUST WANTING SOME SICK CLEAR LENSES OR NEEDING TO REPLACE THEIR OLD FOGGED UP LENSES WITH SOME SICK CLEAR LENSES LIKE THESE!!!!!! Hope this helps someone else out✌

P.S.
Sorry for the 2 crappy blurry pictures but the day I took them the sun was out shining right into the camera on my phone and they are the best I could get!!!! I will post some better pictures when I get my truck back from the body shop!!!! I installed a new roll pan in place of the old bumper and am having the whole truck repainted in a sick baby blue color I picked out!!!! I just can't wait to get it back from the body shop, it's gonna look so sexy with that new roll pan and being freshly painted in that beautiful baby blue color I picked out instead of the factory Victory red it's been forever!!!! Will update pictures as soon as I get her back, thank's all✌!!!!

top_rant.text:
This is a Replacement of my old 7 year old cover. Also a Bedlocker, but the motor no longer worked and the remotes were beat up. I needed a new cover. I like this type for a number of reasons. The Plus on this NEW version over my OLD version, even though they are both pace Edwards Bedlocker, there are some differences.

The New one has NO LIGHT on it like my old one did!!! I kind of like the light, but I guess it could get broken if you had large cargo in the back of your truck and it was hitting against the back of the Canister where the light was.

On the other hand the New Canister is a little smaller then my old one, and it does have a Release Lever on it. You can then crawl under the cover, Activate the level Manually and then Manually Roll the Cover Open and close in a Emergency if for some reason the Cover didn't open on it's own. My Old cover, I was out of luck!!! No such feature.

The Bedlocker covers open and close by remote Control!!! You can stop it at any point you wish!!! Open it a little, or most of the way. The Old remote was a 4 button, Open/Close and one was for the Light, Nice! The New ones are a 2 button design (Open/Close). My only problem with them is that it's kind of LARGE in size for a remote!!! It doesn't need to be as big, is should be half the size!!!

The Cover Receiving unit that picks up the remote and controls the cover is now inside the Canister about the middle of it, on my old one it was outside, placed next to it! The new way looks cleaner, but I see electronic exposed on the back side, the side the water goes into. How will this hold up?!?! Don't know at this time.

I also got the Manual Switch Option. It's a Extra Kit. About $80!!! This allowed me to Install a toggle switch in my truck on the Dash in a empty area. I can Open and Close my cover using that instead of only the Remote. Nice!!! I had wanted to do that with my old cover, but never got around to it. Also for what the kit is, it's kind of expensive!!! It's easiest to install it at the same time as the cover, as it Plugs into one of the 2 connectors at the Canister. It's harder to run the wire to the front of your truck once the canister is down in place. I didn't have it at the time, and so had to do it the hard way and fish it, but I managed. You run the Main power cable for the Canister up to the front where I tapped into the Main power supply at the fuse Box under the hood of my Chevy there on the left side, the switch, I ran to the front just the same way, but under the hood I went into the CAB though one of the rubber Plugs. The cable wasn't quite long enough for my needs, so I had to extend it just a bit to reach where I placed the switched.

Also to lock the Tail Gate, I had when I got my old cover, a Powered Tail gate lock. When I unlock my Driver door the Tail gate also Unlocks, when I lock my door, the tail gate it locked! It's pretty simple how it works, but to get it working correctly can be a pain! Because my Chevy has a Class 2 Interface I believe is what it's called, there was no way to just TAP into the power lock system of my Chevy truck. I had to run the wire into my truck, the same place the switch wire goes though, then removing the door Paneling and side floor cover, gain access to the rubber the covers the wires from the door Jam to the Door, and fish the lock wires though there and connect up where the Door Power lock wires are Located!!! Bypassing the Class 2 Interface and connecting directly at that Location. If I had it to do over again, I might have run the cable though the Passenger side Instead!! That way the tailgate only worked when I double press the remote unlock button to unlock BOTH doors, not just the driver door with one push. Less wear and tear that way on the tail gate motor. Not a big deal, still working after 7 years, and can easily throw in a new one if that one fails. Wire is after all already run. It is a little tricky fishing the wire out the bottom of your tailgate and in a good spot!!!

My old cover held up 7 years!!!The Black on the Aluminum Roll cover was starting to peel in a few places!!! Of course once again the motor failed and so I couldn't open the cover, just the tailgate. On the Plus side. I LOVE how with a push of a botton, Open up the back of your truck completely!!! Yes the Canister takes a little space up front, but not much, and it's open under it for the most part other then a drain tube on each end which I have draining out though small holes in the front corners of my truck bed. There was drain holes already there, but I did have to increase the size just a bit larger. When Rain falls on the cover, it drains into the Canister, and then out through the 2 drain pipes, one on each side!!! When I was at Best Buy for example, and they Price Matched my 50" HDTV, I got it right then, I just rolled up the cover and loaded and strapped it down. Can't do that with one of them Solid hard Top's which you'd have to take OFF and place somewhere . Can take two people to do, and you need to be careful not to scratch that top. Oh and placed where you hope no one will steal it!!! It's also made out of Aluminum Panels, so it's pretty strong. Can hold up Snow and whatnot. You can't Walk on it, maybe a kid.

You'll also get a slight Mileage Improvement with a cover. Something that time and again doesn't work opening and leaving open your Tailgate!!! You in fact get worse mileage, and when you tailgate is left open for a long period, the bed side rails tend to expend out over time. When you go and try closing your tailgate when you need it, it now no longer closes right. Myth Buster also did the whole tailgate test which once again proved leaving the tail gate open was worse then closed!!! You wouldn't think it, but in fact it does, and when it's explained, it does make sense!!!

Anyone who owns a truck knows that people like to throw their garbage in the back of your truck!!! I guess it's better then the ground, but quite rude. Kind of hard to do when your bed is covered. When people can't see what's in the back of your truck because of the cover, they don't go and steal it. Generally with a cover and a lock tail gate, people will just move on to something easier, and that they can SEE to make it worth it.

Pace Edwards also makes a Manual version. Not as good to me. The Panels I think are a Plastic I believe on it, but it's like half the price! You can also get side Rails that instead of being low profile and flat, you can get ones with a slot down them. This allows accessory to be mounted on the rails like Cargo Containers, Latter racks, Bike Rack's, etc. Go to [...] and find out more.

When I tow my Jetski, I can't see my trailer when the ski is off in the water and I have to backup down the ramp, It's almost impossible. Yet, Roll up the cover like half way and drop the tailgate, now I can easily see the trailer and backup without a problem. Nice!! Can't do that with a Solid cover, or with a Push of a button, but I can!!!
top_rant.title: I really like this type of cover

top_rant.text:
Oh lord this thing has some controversy around it! Just like one of the top reviews of this product mentioned, I myself was a little cautious about this little generator. Here's a MAJOR tip to prevent any of the failures the more negative reviews mentions, please read the manual! It specifies everything that the generator CAN and CANNOT do! I repeat, this generator cannot save the entire world! But it can save parts of yours! That rant out of the way, let's get into the products strengths and weaknesses in a fairly unorganized review. (Sorry ahead of time!)

Strengths and Weakness:
= In general it is a economically efficient, jack of all trades, master of none type of deal. Yes it has many helpful features, but they aren't as great as tools DEDICATED to that one specific task. For example, the air-compressor to re-inflate tires or inflatable beds. A dedicated air-compressor would do both of those things relatively quickly. While the Rally Generator can inflate those two things, it won't be as fast as the dedicated air-compressor. In other words, it is what it is. It CAN do those things. Just not as quickly as tools dedicated to that one and only specific task.

= Again with the "master of none" type of situation we have going on here, the battery capacity, for a generator (granted, a very low priced generator, under $100 USD is very impressive actually!), is relatively modest. From personal testing, my smartphone can get about 4 or so charges before the generator reaches a RED battery status (I have a 10,000 MaH battery bank and it roughly charges my phone about 4 times for comparison sake). Speaking of which, when it is in the RED, try to charge it as soon as possible! Which leads me to a a main oversight that leads to the negative reviews,
despite being rechargeable, DO NOT OVERCHARGE IT! Here's what's going to happen when you charge this device. It will be a RED status indicator. When it eventually turns GREEN, STOP CHARGING IT. If you do, you will damage the overall capacity of the battery! If you do that, battery capacity wise, you should be problem free. This is mentioned in the condense manual they provide you with the generator (or at least with mine).

While I'm at it, a second user-error-based issue, the hand-crank. According to the manual let the hand-crank's rope/string piece RETRACT ALL THE WAY BEFORE PULLING AGAIN! Here's what I do, pull it firmly, then hold on to it while it reels itself back in. Once it is almost all the way reeled in, let go completely. Then repeat the cycle. Another quirk to be aware of, DO NOT EXCEED THE HAND GENERATOR PULL-HANDLE MAXIMUM CORD LENGTH OF 30 INCHES! Again, in the manual. In other words, while pulling and letting go, and so on, do not pull too far essentially. Short, and firm pulls basically! Not strong arming it like a lawn mower! Oh god no don't do that! If you do as the manual says, you should be doing fine so far.

Next up on the controversial list of problems, it outright says what it can and can't do in the manual. The AC outlet's power inverter is 140 watts. So don't expect it to power a (regular) fridge for example! Its 140 watts. It won't power a house!

Below the AC outlet, is a USB port that is 5 Volts DC and 2.1 Amps. Basically charge low powered USB devices with it. Smart phones, and tablets for example. If you have the proper cable(s) for it, even a Nintendo Switch! Yes it can charge most console controllers in general too! Since most of them love to charge by USB ports. Great for those extended grid-down/blackouts! They generally shouldn't happen often, but when they do, this little generator will make those occurrences a little more tolerable!

Before we go on, there's only three real ways to charge this generator, with the included AC charger, the included DC charger (for when you have it with you in the car and want to charge it via the car's DC outlet), and hand-cranking it up! SOLAR IS NOT ONE OF THEM! Again, it isn't a $100+ (USD) generator. Don't expect THAT much!

Speaking of charging, when the battery eventually reaches RED status, charging it via the AC charger, it will take quite a few hours! Not exactly 24 hours, but it isn't 10 minutes I'll tell you that much! Again MAKE SURE YOU UNPLUG IT WHEN THE STATUS INDICATOR REACHES GREEN. Do it ASAP! In other words, be on constant standby while it charges!

As for hand-cranking it. Its slow. It works, but like with most hand-cranks, for most situations you probably won't need to use it unless you want to charge it a little bit at a time instead of in one relentless stream. In this case, while hand-crank charging, remember to stop and press the battery indicator button to check if it is in the GREEN or not! It is right above the DC outlet, and below the work-light! This is because while hand-cranking charging it, the indicator light will not be active like how it would while being plugged into the wall for example! By the way, to the immediate right of the battery indicator light is where you plug in the AC or DC charging cables to charge the generator that way. THIS IS VERY IMPORTANT, WAIT AT LEAST 1 HOUR BEFORE RECHARGING IT. So if you used it that day, wait an hour for it to cool off before charging it in any of the three viable ways to charge it! If you didn't use it that day or in the last 1 or more, then you're safe to do so! Don't forget to pull the hand-crank in a relatively straight line!

The generator comes with 3 stems for the air-compressor. According to the manual they're as follows:
* Small Inflatable Stem
* Ball stem
* and Large Inflatable Stem

Now for the LED (Work) Light and the Emergency Flasher. It doesn't have a DEDICATED button for each! Instead, much like a cheap flashlight, it will CYCLE between those two modes. So if you want the blinking emergency lights, press the button until they start flashing instead. Basically literally turn it off and on until you get the lighting setting you want. No the work light won't light up the entire house. Yes it will be relatively useful in a small room should a blackout/grid-down situation occur. Yes it will help finding your way in the dark outside, etc. Again, a dedicated lamp or high-powered flashlight would do this all better, but "all in one" types of tools/gadgets more than likely means compromising somewhere! This should be common sense, but don't look directly into the LED (work/emergency) light either!

Again, manual already states this, but store it in a dry, open, room-temperature area with a cover on top of it! PRO TIP, it comes with a plastic bag that covers it. You can use that to cover it when not in use, or if you NEED to use it in the rain for some reason! Just make sure its resting on a reasonable surface! It isn't water proof!

Same deal with the jump starter. Dedicated ones will do it faster and better, but if you're hard pressed for space, or you just want one tool to do many things and not multiple tools to do specific things, then this generator is great for being that ONE tool! But again, read the manual, do not deviate from the manual EXCEPT WHEN IT SAYS TO CHARGE IT FOR 24 HOURS WHEN YOU FIRST GET IT. Mine came partially charged. So it took barely 7 hours to get it to green! I reckon that's where most of the negative reviews came from. But even then the manual even states "CAUTION: Do not overcharge the unit!" and, "Note: The device may arrive partially charges from the manufacturer. It is recommended to fully charge the device immediately after purchasing and before using it for the first time".

Honestly just print out a huge amount of copies of the manual and bring a few copies with you everywhere! If i have the time to, I'll see if I can upload any photos of my manual. Before using mine, I immediately read through all of that (which is only 4 pages so no excuses!), and I underlined or put red pen indicators to make it quick on what I should always keep in mind for the features I use the most. The manual is easily the most important part of this product! Keep it, understand it, produce as many copies as possible to avoid future issues and confusion, etc! Don't underestimate the manual and this generator will serve you very well!

I think I covered everything? Lets see, hand crank, check, air compressor, check, jump starter, check, DC outlet, AC outlet, USB outlet, LED work/emergency light, the battery indicator and the generator's charging hole, check!

I will put the specifications here in case you want to know before you purchase one. Again, they're on the manual too in case you do get one and want to confirm something for future reference.

Specifications:
* Built-in Battery: 8-amp hour (Ah) sealed, lead-acid, "maintenance-free (in the Winter, every two months when not in use, recharge it. In the Summer, every month when not in use, recharge it)", spill-proof, rechargeable.
* DC outlet: 12 Volt DC (15 Amp replaceable blade fuse)
* USB Port: 5 Volt(s) DC/ 2.1 A(mps)
* LED light: 12 Volt DC / 330 mA
* Power inverter: 140 Watt(s)
* Air Compressor: 150 PSI (15 Amp replaceable blade fuse)
* Circuit breaker: Internal, Auto-reset (after 60 seconds)
* Net Weight: 10.6 lbs. (AKA Pounds)
* Unit Dimensions: 9.14 inches (height), 6.97 inches (width), and 12.2 inches (depth.
* Storage temperature: (-4 degrees F to 140 degrees F) (-20 degrees C to 60 degrees C). Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated area with overhead cover (AKA probably the plastic bag it came with that conveniently can be used for that)!
* Operation temperature: (32 degrees F to 104 degrees F) ( 0 degrees C to 40 degrees C). Operate in a dry, well-ventilated area and place device on a leveled surface.

Forgot if I mentioned this but, when charging via any method that isn't hand-cranking, the battery indicator DOES NOT go from RED, AMBER, TO GREEN. It literally goes from RED to GREEN. So when charging it, be sure to be on constant stand buy!

Okay I think that should be everything. If not, I'll try my best to answer any reasonable questions should I have the time to do so. If you do buy one of these, best of luck and happy reading as you're gonna NEED to do the ladder! Reading the manual is non-negotiable. If you ain't gonna read it, don't buy this! If you're going to, by all means go ahead!
top_rant.title: STELLAR if you know what you're getting yourself into(basically read the manual and it will be fine)

top_rant.text:
A guy named Matt had posted the following 1 star review for AirStop;
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
ByMatt Pavelleon May 1, 2015
Package Quantity: 1Style Name: SVerified Purchase
I tried to use this on a small (1cm) long tear on my Aerobed (actual Aerobed brand bed - not a generic) - which I should be (by all literature I've read) made of vinyl. This compound actually dissolved the vinyl! The tear grew larger and more jagged as applied the compound... no idea why.
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
As I began to answer his question I decided to fashion the answer as a review instead. I have meant to do this review for the last 4 years and never got to it, so here goes;
Matt, I’ll tell you why the hole grew larger and did not hold. But l am goin;

1. AirStop is predominantly used on pinhile type leaks. With those leaks this stuff is nothing short of being the greatest miracle since the birth of you know who. I say this with the experience of 35 years owning a business that for the last 15 years included air mattress and white water repairs. I have probably done over 200. BTW, sometimes, NO MATTER WHAT, one cannot get ahead of leak and there is no choice but to stop resuccitating and start harvesting organs. Large patches of vinyl, large plugs of flocked vinyl, pumps, valves. corners etc. I have been successful at Dr. Frankenstein, radical rebuilds that involved replacinf complete 10" x 10" corners which was really like manufacturing one. These REALLY radical repairs have held until today (I don't send them back out after a repair unless it is fixed for life. By that, I mean that I won't put them back with their owners unless I am certain that the repaired section is more durable than an actual solid wall. There might be failured in the future of the bed but never at the repair point. That's because when I am done the area I repaired is actuallu much stronger than the rest of the bed. I have seen some of my patients come back to me after staying in service for a few years after my original repair and the bed has stretched and stretched but my repair patch still remains there at its original size. This actually becomes a problem after many years because it acts like a wood knot or better comparison would be it is similar to human scar tissue. It just does not stretch with the rest of the skin. At this point though people are thrilled they had it so long and just brought the beds back to show mebizarre results and to pass it along as an organ donor.

At the same time, I have had pin holes that were so difficult to find that it took over an hour and SEVERAL sweeps of the mattress with a soapy spray bottle and had to put my heavy nephew on the mattress as well to force air out of the hole so it could be found. I have had a couple I had to combine spray bottles, big nephews and medical grade stethescopes to locate the hole. These holes were on solid vinyl not flocked, and not near seams or even high load points. And when I finally did find these microscopic holes no matter what I did I could not stop the leak. That includes being persistent and trying the repair from the pinhole stage and working outward applying larger and larger patches. It is variance in materials down to the molecular level that either fid not act well with its molecular neighbors or were actually harmful little variants. Sort of the cancer cells of vinyl. The point is that no method can be declared to ALWAYS work or NEVER work because for different people in different climates and different body weights that bough matresses from different sellers who stored theit matressses in different sea levels and delivered them in different trucks and were unboxed by… well, you get the point.

So while there is nothing foolproof, and on some repairs a piece of scotch tape will hold for a year (yep, I’ve seen it), there in one thing that is constant and undeniable in my coparatively enormous sample size of air matress repairs, and that is the fact that this exact product, the little tiny Coghlan’s Air Stop in the Green Bottle is vastly, far and away, undeniably, without question, most assuredly, absolutely, fo’ sho’ EXPONENTIALLY more successful in successful, permanent repairs than ANY other method. Exponentially better than the cheap kit that comes with the matress and all the way up the line to exponentially better than any $100 REI field repair kit. But I have NO DOUBT he thinks he reinvented the wheel. I believe most of you reading this have been utterly flummoxed by stubborn repairs the almost, nearly, practically held. You were SURE it held but the next morning that dreaded pliability softened when you poked it with your finger. Likely that these sociopaths would have snapped over something else eventually but the point is that I am SURE people have died at the hands of someone deep into an Air Matress repair that’s not going well!!

Getting back to the response to the poster I was crafting to her before I decided to make a more inlusive revire of AirStop. We have determined that this stuff is the holy water of AirMatress repairs one MUST follow 2 basic rules and never vary, If this is not followed then you have relegated this liquid Nirvana to also-rand status.

A tear MUST be fixed with a patch. Always, 100% of the time.
A pinhole MUST be reaired with NO patch. Just the free floating application method. No patch even if the hole is on a seam. If you stick with these general rules then the rest is actually rather forgiving. I have used heavy duty Ziploc plastic bags for patches (in the field with no other materisl), and the repair has hel through several subsequent trips I am told. A guy named Matt had posted the following 1 star review for AirStop;
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
Matt Pavelleon May 1, 2015
I tried to use this on a small (1cm) long tear on my Aerobed (actual Aerobed brand bed - not a generic) - which I should be (by all literature I've read) made of vinyl. This compound actually dissolved the vinyl! The tear grew larger and more jagged- as applied the compound... no idea why.
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
As I began to answer his question I decided to fashion the answer as a review instead. I have meant to do this review for the last 4 years and never got to it, so here goes;

--------------------------------------
Matt, I’ll tell you why the hole grew larger and did not hold. But I am going to digress first.

First, AirStop is King with nearly 100% success rate at repairing predominantly pinhole and rounded jab type holes causing your leak. With those leaks this stuff is nothing short of being the greatest miracle since the birth of you know who. I say this with the experience of 35 years running a foundation which among other things rehabilitated Air Mattresses almost from the beginning. In the past 10 years it became a specialty which led us last year placing our 2,000th donated or rehabilitated Air Mattress to housing authorities to redistribute nationwide. Running the foundation has meant that I have had hands on or witnessed over 1,000 bags (our term for the mattress). Further, my nephew supplies and organizes a large white water club and I have seen or helped with a couple of dozen large, white water bag repairs. If bag rehabilitation were like the NFL I am sure that I might be one of, if not THE top player in the league and I would be among the Top 3 highest paid baggers. To say I am a shoe in to be among the first round of inductees to the bag Hall Of Fame would not be an overstatement, but rather a proclamation of fact. Sorry to sound like I am a braggart but it is important that I convey how much time and experience I have in the Bag trenches. It’s even possible that if Bagging were actually a respected profession or sport that Queen Elizabeth herself would agree that as far as Bags go, I would be a Royal. She would probably Knight me like she did Richard Branson or David Attenborough based solely on the acknowledgement of said Royalty.

OK, enough about me for now. At least now if you leave any comments you will know to be respectful and referring to me as Sir would be acceptable – actually, not acceptable, but preferable, got it?
Before anything else I want to clear up one fact.
Fact: NO MATTER WHAT method you use, there will be a certain amount of Bags that will never hold air properly again. This means that a failure of a particular method or product such as AirStop is NOT necessarily an indication that the method or products are failures. Bad things happen to good Bags sometimes and the finest surgeons and elixirs were never going to work anyways. This is a factor of both Mother Nature and probably to a MUCH greater level a combination of Evil and planned obsolescence which we will discuss another time. However, in many of these inoperable cases, if you as the practitioner cannot EVER get ahead of leak, then you need to remain composed and do the right thing! Now is not the time for grief. There will be ample opportunity to mourn later and you need to remember that there are pallets of other Bags just waiting their turns for you. So just to Call It. Your only viable option is to stop resuscitating and start harvesting Bag organs.

Specifically, SLICE out Large patches of body and side vinyl, large plugs of flocked vinyl, pumps, and valves, corners etc. I have been successful at Dr. Frankenstein, radical rebuilds that involved replacing complete 10" x 10" corners which was really like manufacturing one. 100% of the time that someone has touched base with me after one of these miraculous resections, I have been told these REALLY radical repairs have held until today (I don't send them back out after a repair unless it is fixed for life). By that, I mean that I won't put them back with their original owners or send them on to meet their new foster parents unless I am certain that the repaired section is more durable than an actual solid wall. There might be failure in the future of the bed but never at the repair point. That's because when I am done the area I repaired is actually much stronger than the rest of the bed. I have seen some of my patients come back to me after staying in service for a few years after my original repair and the bed has stretched and stretched but my repair patch still remains there at its original size. This actually becomes a problem after many years because it acts like a wood knot or better comparison would be it is similar to human scar tissue. It just does not stretch with the rest of the skin. As its body goes through the normal elderly process of stretching and sagging it remains taut and supple. This makes for a very ugly Bag with big puffy bellies that are pinched and restricted by patches. The very best analogy is that the patches are the hull and the bags are the big White and Yellow popcorn outer part. The “problem” is that it is hard to sleep on popcorn so many times the very patches that let them live also contributed to several years of prolonged deformity and eventual death.
Honestly, I do love when one of these come back in the door. At that point people are so thrilled they had it so long and just brought the beds back to show me bizarre results and to pass it along as an organ donor.

So there are those radical reconstruction surgeries but on the opposite end is what I alluded to earlier. We have had pin holes that were so difficult to find that it took over an hour and SEVERAL sweeps of the mattress with a soapy spray bottle and had to put my heavy nephew on the mattress as well to force air out of the hole so it could be found. I have had many that in order to locate the wound we had to combine spray bottles, big nephews and medical grade stethoscopes to locate the hole. These holes were on solid vinyl not flocked, and not near seams or even high load points. And when we finally did find these microscopic holes no matter what we did, we could not stop the leak. That includes being persistent and trying the repair from the pinhole stage and working outward applying larger and larger patches. When we hit a seam, I stop and call the time of death just as I instructed you guys earlier to know when to just give up because it ain’t gonna work (yes, we actually call it - long, funny story on how that started that I won't get into but to this day we act out the bereaved family and dejected Doctor while harvesting organs and cleaning up)
Over the years I learned from science friends - as the manufacturers of these don't know squat about them - that these occasional of outliers that could not be saved despite the fact that the damage was microscopic were simply cases of a bunch of molecules that were destined to come together and be crappy simply because the nature of nature is just like that sometimes. The point is that no method can be declared to ALWAYS work or NEVER work because for different people in different climates and different body weights that bough mattresses from different sellers who stored their mattresses in different sea levels and delivered them in different trucks and were unboxed by… well, you get the point.

So while there is nothing foolproof because not all bags can even be fixed, on some other repairs a piece of scotch tape will hold for a year (yep, I’ve seen it). there is one thing that is constant and undeniable in my comparatively enormous sample size of air mattress repairs, and that is the fact that this exact product, the little tiny Coghlan’s Air Stop in the Green Bottle is vastly, far and away, undeniably, without question, most assuredly, absolutely, fo’ sho’ EXPONENTIALLY more successful in successful, permanent repairs than ANY other method. I’m talking 90% fived withExponentially better than the cheap kit that comes with the mattress and all the way up the line to exponentially better than any $100 REI field repair kit. Every DIY hack you have ever seen on the internet for sure may have worked perfectly for that person so they will declare for example that the “Walmart Inner tube repair kit #4 with rubber Schwinn 3cm patches finished with a glaze of Loctite double compound rubber epoxy is GUARANTEED to work!” Well that’s just plain human bias at work. But I have NO DOUBT he thinks he reinvented the wheel. I believe most of you reading this have been utterly flummoxed by stubborn repairs the almost, nearly, practically held. You were SURE it held but the next morning that dreaded pliability softened when you poked it with your finger. I know many of us did not even want to poke it the next day because they just could not handle it if the air mattress was not firm. I also have NO DOUBT that people have killed family members in domestic arguments gone bad because after hours, perhaps days, they either could not get the tiniest of holes fixed or they woke up during the night sinking after they thought it was fixed and family or a friend said something the wrong way or burned a piece of toast and that set off a fit of rage that led to a homicide. Likely that these sociopaths would have snapped over something else eventually but the point is that I am SURE people have died at the hands of someone deep into an Air Mattress repair that’s not going well!!
Getting back to the response to the poster I was crafting to her before I decided to make a more inclusive review of AirStop, here i. We have determined that this stuff is the holy water of Air Mattress repairs one MUST follow 2 basic rules and never vary, if this is not followed then you have relegated this liquid Nirvana to also-rand status.
This entire wall of text can be summarized with the following tips/instructions;
A tear MUST be fixed with a patch. Forget what the directions say about using AirStop without a patch for small tears. Always, 100% of the times use it for ALL tears. The simple way to know whether it is a tear or a hole is for you to look at the hole. If the naked human eye can see any sort of line vertical or horizontal line in the wound. If your naked eye can’t see a separation like a tear, then and only then can this non-linear, rounded wound be considered a hole and just plain AirStop with no patch.
A pinhole MUST be repaired with NO patch. Just dab and apply the AirStop freely and direct on the hole and about 1/8th of an inch out from the center of the hole. For your initial repair do NOT use a patch even if the hole is on a seam. If you stick with these general rules then the rest is actually rather forgiving. I have used heavy duty Ziploc plastic bags for patches at a family reunion (in the field with no other material available), and the repair has held through several subsequent trips I am told. Tying this all back to Matt and his mostly existential pondering of “why”
>>>>>>>>>>>>
ByMatt Pavelleon May 1, 2015

Very simple, Matt. You did not use a patch.

top_rant.text:
******* UPDATE FOLLOWING INITIAL REVIEW, UPGRADED FROM 2 STARS TO 4 STARS WITH CAVEAT ******

This review will cover the headlight units and their assembly, as well as some tips for those installing the headlights themselves.

Pros:
nice looks
bright halos (when they have warmed up)
both low beams and hi beams can be adjusted up and down
holes lined up perfectly for assembly
decent quality plastics used

Cons:
didn't come with quick connectors
assembly issues with the LEDs discovered, but ultimately fixed
cannot adjust lights left and right (hot spots point to the right too much)
'hot spot' pattern, uneven illumination (very poor engineering of projectors)

Overall:
Disappointed. All looks and no substance. Owning these is like dating an air-headed hottie that can't cook - fun to have on your arm around town, but she's gonna burn the house down if she tries to make a bowl of cereal.

The light pattern on these are more than disappointing, and may even be dangerous. Projectors only work when there aren't hot spots. Good projectors have an even, wide, and bright pattern. The not-so-bright spots are 95% of the pattern with these. Worse, you cannot adjust the focus left or right. The hot spots on mine focus too far to the right, so I'm stuck with this pattern until I get something else (more on this later). The light output from the stock headlights is CONSIDERABLY better.

I had to put HIDs on these to make them closer to the light output of the stock headlights. I installed a 35 Watt HID kit with these. Have both 6000K and 4300K bulbs, and recommend 4300K (more lumens) - in case you were wondering which to get. I may get the 50 Watt Morimoto HID kit with 4300K. Apparently you can get almost 1000 lumen more light from those, compared to the 35W model (a heavy duty wiring harness is needed for the 50W ones, though). But then again, the hot spots may make all of this a moot point.

Also had some issues with the LEDs on these units (intermittent On/Off), but a quick look revealed that the assemblers didn't bend the leads on the LEDs correctly - so it was a quick fix after my initial F-bomb fit.

This is a negative on Nissan, not Spyder - Adjusting the hi beams are easy, but adjusting the low beams is a pain. I had to keep the two easy-to-get-to bolts off in order to squeeze my arm and tool from the front (stubby ratchet with 8mm socket). Other's recommend going through the wheel well, a backdoor approach, so all the bolts stay on during up/down adjustments.

Conclusion:
Buy a quality HID kit, like Morimoto (not sold on Amazon), if you buy these headlights. This will improve the function to a reasonable/usable level.

My belated research has uncovered that these headlights aren't any better or worse than other aftermarket custom headlights, when it comes to light output performance. They're all made in China, and ALL have crappy projectors.

These headlights have some really good points, like, quality materials, fit and finish, and cool looks. So I have to give more than 1 star. However, their primary function as a device to illuminate the night is so far off the mark that I can't give it more than 2 stars.

In the future, I may actually retrofit my old headlights with a projector retrofit kit. This is considerably more work, but I'm a little night blind, so I need all the lumens I can get. These Spyder headlight will have to remain on while I do more research and save for a retrofit kit. The 4300K HID kit has made it much more usable, so I'm not too worried about keeping these on for now.

Still interested in buying these? Then the following should help make assembly a little easier...

ASSEMBLY HINTS:
!!!!KEEP YOUR OLD HEADLIGHTS!!!! You'll be canabalizing some things from them!
-buy quick connectors
-watch SEVERAL You Tube vids - search "Titan Spyder Headlight"
-turn on old headlights and mark (with tape) where the light pattern hits the wall on the garage
-remove the front grill per videos (very easy)
-turn steering wheel all the way to the right or left (this opens up the wheel well area)
-remove screw in middle of wheel well, located in front middle of well, at an even height with headlight
(you may need to poke your head in the wheel well to see it)
-bend away the plastic wheel wells to get to the hidden side bolts (one 8mm, one 10mm, each side)
-be sure to put those hidden bolts back, or your fender will bulge later, not kidding
(big middle finger to Nissan for that engineering marvel)
-you'll transplant the wire harness from the old headlights
-AND you may also have to transplant those little metal threaded bolt holder clamps
(don't worry, you'll know what those are when you try to put the 10mm bolt back on without them)
-to put the grill back on, you need to use needle nose pliers to remove the plastic clips that stayed on the frame when you removed it, and place them on the grill before trying to put the grill back on...this allows the grill to snap back on to the frame very easily

TOOLS NEEDED:
-painter's tape (to mark top of old light pattern)
-8mm & 10mm sockets and wrenches
-stubby rachet
-stubby phillips head screwdriver (for wheel well)
-VERY long flat tip screw driver, like 14 inches (to remove the grill)
-needle nose pliers and/or wire crimpers
-electrical tape
-16/18 gauge wire quick connects
***if you want a quality job done***
*medium duty solder iron (you want something over 900 Watts)
*wire shrink wrap (various sizes)
*heat gun or cigarette lighter
*0.003" diameter no-clean lead-free solder

Best of luck. If you have any questions about this specific product, or the assembly, post a question and I'll answer as promptly as I can.

*** UPDATE 2/4/2013 (few weeks later) ***

I decided to buy the parts for a retrofit piecemeal, but after installing the Morimoto HID kit, I won't be retrofitting the old headlights after all.

I recently received the Morimoto HID kit, and decided to install to see if they performed as well as testimonials on HID forums said they did. Well, all I can say is "OMFG why didn't I do this sooner?"

Yep, my opinion of these headlights has dramatically changed now that I have a Japanese HID kit, instead of the cheapie Chinese HID kit that are found on Amazon and eBay. As it turns out, the length and straightness of bulb are just as important as the quality of the ballasts.

Certainly, the Japanese ballasts are a world apart from the Chinese ballasts, and I don't want to discount how important this is. It is just important to note that the projectors on these headlights are tuned for high quality HID bulbs, which have specific dimensions. After swapping out the Chinese kit with the Japanese kit, the pinpointed hot spots (that were off to the right) were replaced with a broad bright pattern that was a difference of night and day.

In essence, the sweet spot inside the projector body is specifically made for a quality HID bulb, which is basically longer and straighter than the cheapie Chinese bulb - and very much longer than the original Halogen bulb. I was shocked at the difference in light output - eyes-bulged-jaw-dropped-SHOCK. And after carefully reading up on how projectors work, especially the importance on the dimensions of the bulbs, I have come to appreciate the engineering effort put into these headlights. Sure there are better projectors available for retrofit, but these are actually quite good. And with a quality Japanese HID kit, they are significantly brighter than the stock headlights, which was my goal to begin with.

In case you were wondering where I bought and how much I spent:
TheRetrofitSource dot com
1 x 9006: Morimoto Elite HID System (Elite9006) = $150.00
Wire Harness Type Heavy Duty Relay
Wire Harness 9006
Kelvin 4300K
Ballasts Morimoto 3Five (35w)

After shipping it was about $163

I spent $79 on the cheapie ones from OmegaXenon dot com, which turned out to be more expensive than the ones on eBay or Amazon, but just at crappy. So don't be a fool, as I was. You get what you pay for with HID. Now I'm not saying to buy an expensive HID kit. I'm saying to buy a name-brand quality kit from a reputable source - 'reputable source' being the most important part of this recommendation. Also, watch out for phony Philips HID kits on eBay.

I also highly recommend you do NOT get 55w ballasts. Those will eventually burn out the mirrors of the internal reflectors and possibly melt the plastic housing and wiring, requiring you to replace the entire headlight assembly (and wiring harness). The 35w ones are plenty bright enough. Trust me on this. I am partially night blind, and I love the 35w output.

I only gave 4 stars, due to the fact that you have to buy a quality HID kit to make these function properly.

See bottom for the new review on my SECOND starter! NEW!!! UPDATE!!! 8-19-14 (1-24-2015 See last update at the bottom!)

(This is my older review, of the one that actually works!)
While I haven't actually used this product yet in a real world scenario, I have charged it up and tested it, and from what I have seen, it appears to be something that all that have already bought says it to be. I tested this by running a DC fan and a USB charger. The battery jumper I have not tested.
It appears to be made solid, of course, there's a heavy (not too heavy though) battery inside that contributes to the ruggedness/heftiness of it. All of the gauges seem to work fine
The light, which is NOT a LED bulb, is bright enough for an emergency, I'd expect. The light claims to be positionable, and it is, but only a VERY SLIGHT distance, AND, it will not stay in that position, you would have to hold it there, which is really odd to me. That's not a really bad thing though, you could easily move the box itself so that the light is where you need it.
The bulb not being a LED though, that is very strange in this day and time, there may be a fix by using a bulb replacement that is a LED, many companies have them, I just have not checked that out yet. In a unit like this where ALL of the power is a precious commodity, you'd think that'd be one of the number one things they'd do in the design stages. Actually, there are not many of these boxes from other makers that have them though, maybe only the high-dollar ones have the LED.

Also, from what I read before purchasing, this unit is the exact same as the Allstart Marine Jump Starter, it's just in a different color. (It makes it sound like it's weather-proof or something) I bring this up really so that people that read the reviews know that while this camouflage unit gets almost a perfect score, the BLUE Marine unit does not, oddly enough, so read carefully if you are on the fence about either one. I may be completely wrong on this too, I have only read that these two are identical inside, I have not done any scientific tests or done the research myself, but you can if you are interested, I am only bringing it up in case it matters.

As soon as I have some use from it, I'll come back and give a review on that, until then, this is all I can really do.
The reason I give this unit only 4 stars is that it hasn't been tested in a real-life situation yet, other than that, there is no reason, I'd give it a 5 star rating.

Too bad though, I liked the first one fine, but this second one I bought, it came in a box that was not packed well at all, and was possibly damaged en route. Oh, wait, like what they just sold me? I double-checked too, this comes 'from Amazon', not Cal-Van, Amazon shows this very plainly. They can ship out flammable and gaseous merchandise, but they can't have it returned... Crazy!
Anyway, what happened is, when I plugged it in, there is a high-pitched noise that will drive you crazy, the kind that a bad power strip will make when it goes bad. This is definitely coming from the inverter too, you can flip off the switch and the noise stops.

The help light comes on, and I would imagine this may actually charge too, but I don't want this if the inverter doesn't work, so it'll go back.

Let me make this clear, I am not sure who's at fault here, if it's shoddy workmanship in the making of it, or the shipper (Amazon) who had someone that doesn't know, or care, how to pack a box with something that really needs it, and that weighs 25lbs.. It came in a box from the manufacturer, and that box was placed inside a larger box that doesn't fit the original box perfectly, so they need to add packing around it. They kind of did, there was a wrinkled piece of brown packing paper laying on top of the box, not helping a thing at all. The empty space that could've used some packing to help keep it from sliding all over, it had nothing. So, the entire box actually was too small, and the packing was just plain stupid. Any person that had a half a brain would know that this wouldn't help, using a little piece of paper to cush the shipping? That person needs to get some real training, then fired... I say, "fired" because this person knew, and didn't care, anyone would know not to let a box go out the way this one went out.

To say I am disappointed is an understatement. This was either never tested at the plant it was made, or Amazon and the 'super shipper' that placed this in a shipping box didn't do their job and it was damaged on the way to my house.
