top_five_star_obscene.text:
Installation of this exhaust was super easy. I get leery every time I see the "insert the flared end over the standard diameter pipe" connections on exhausts like this vs flanges that bolt together, but the flared sections were great on this exhaust. I was fortunate enough to be able to do this on a lift, and I wasn't able to get the large mid-section off without cutting it. There's a YouTube video where a woman takes the stock exhaust off and makes it look like the easiest thing in the world. I call BS on that one. If you're doing this with the truck on jackstands there's no way in h3ll you'll be able to get it off in one piece since it hasn't to be angled so far down to work the back section over the rear axle.

I spent so much time trying to get the damn exhaust hangers off (my truck had 8000 miles on it when I did this, so the rubber was nice and new and sticky on the exhaust hangers), and yes, I bathed them in PB Blaster, which did help. Do yourself a HUGE favor and buy an exhaust hanger removal tool and save tons of time. All in all the removal of the stock exhaust and the installation of this exhaust took me around 2 hours using a lift, and an hour and a half was f'ing with the stupid exhaust hangers trying to lube them, try to find various tools to use as leverage to pry them off, etc. BUY AN EXHAUST HANGER REMOVAL TOOL. Once the exhaust was installed everything was great. There's a bunch of adjustability to move the tips in and out and up and down to get them centered in the bumper cutouts, and if you don't get it right the first time it's pretty simple to crawl under the back of the truck, loosen the clamps, and make some minor adjustments in just a few minutes.

Sound is pretty awesome, but pretty aggressive. If I was someone who had to leave a neighborhood at 6 am every day I'd kinda feel like a knob firing this thing up so early. Your neighbors will hate you. Cold starts are both a sight to behold and an obnoxious affair. I fired it up when my wife was behind it pulling the garbage out of the garage and scared the crap out of her. Totally worth it, but it'll shake the garage door if I'm backed up to it, and if the door open the entire garage itself acts like a giant horn blasting directly at the house across the street, so you really can't win. I leave for work at around 8 AM every morning so I don't feel TOO bad, but I still try to limp it out of the neighborhood on idle. Once you get out on the open road and hammer down it sounds awesome though.

You'll definitely notice the ZF 8 speed gear hunting more than you do with a stock exhaust, but it's not too bad. There's a burp/gurgle when the truck goes into 4 cylinder mode that kinda sounds like crap, but you can disable the cylinder deactivation by hitting the "Max Gear" minus button when in 8th gear (or + button to max it out to 8 when in any other gear) to set the "max gear" setting to 8. Even with the stock exhaust I noticed the change-over from 8 to 4 and it bugs me, so I just do this by habit every time I get into the truck, along with disabling stop-start. Both of these things are an annoyance of the truck though and not the exhaust, but whatever. When out romping on it, it's a badass sounding exhaust. If you're worried about it being too loud, it probably is and you should get the S-Type instead. If you don't give AF, and don't have to leave early for work with neighbors that are within 1/2 mile of you, go for it.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Exhaust sounds amazing... if not a little too aggressive.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Seen some of the other reviews about how hard these are to install. Hopefully I can make this a little easier. Took my time on this install...from opening the box, reading the crappy instructions, getting tools, install itself (with a few bumbles), then checking everything and packing up (including taking the pic below)...45 minutes.

2010 Jeep Wrangler.

Tools:
5mm allen (2)
4mm allen (2)
1/4" socket drive
1/4" extension
1/4" universal joint
1/4 to 3/8 adapter (for me as I didn't have a 13mm 1/4" socket, but did have a 13mm 3/8")
13mm socket (for lower latch nuts)
10mm socket (for upper latch nuts)
Blue Loctite (242) (for the threads of the cross bolt of lower latch(part the holds the actual latch onto the latch mount), when screwing back together you don't want this to loosen over time so I put blue loctite on threads. Blue loctite is light strength removable type, good for this application)
Long slim slotted screwdriver (for prying the plastic center pins up on fasteners that hold the top of grill on(first!), then for prying up the fasteners themselves)
Adj wrench (for tightening the new latch adjustment nuts after install is done..the ones on the center threaded rod)
Gloves (for plugging the turn signal light connectors back on...DO NOT forget this BEFORE you put the grill back on!)
Flashlight (for seeing the damn nuts in the first place and where they are, and how to get to them)
Crappy instructions (worth looking over if you have no technical skill whatsoever)

I have a distinct advantage of having slim fingers, so it was relatively easy to place the socket over the old lower latch nuts and again for the new latch nuts (even to feed the new nuts onto the screw threads). I don't know how to advise if you have fat fingers. after the grill is off, your now working around the engine compartment frame so you've got to get in there somehow to feed the nuts off and on, and fit the socket head.

The old latches came off very easy. The nuts were ridiculously easy to loosen (almost too easy, they weren't even tight), so was able to unthread the screws by hand after breaking the tension with half a turn of the socket wrench. The new latch nuts have the plastic insert to prevent self unthreading, so they were a little harder to get thread on by hand, but once I got a couple turns, they stayed in place to allow the socket to slide over and tighten.

At least if you drop the nuts or tools, they drop down to the ground. It happened a couple times and was thankful I have a jeep...you know, without the big plastic panels covering the underside like most cars and trucks.

A little tip: when installing upper latch, pull down on this piece when setting into position and tightening, so that it is at its lowest position in the hood holes for the screws. The lower latch is going to hook onto this so you don't want it to slip down over time. Slide it to its lowest position and then tighten and the fit will be rock solid.

See pic for the only tools necessary (ref list above). A little common sense and patience and you'll be fine.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Heavy, built like a tank. Easy to install....for me.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This stuff is my go to pesticide. Seriously, this turns you into the reaper on anything with a living cell in it. You decide that it shall be dismissed from this planet, and one finger twitch later, whatever was on the other end is dead on contact. Even if they dodge the shot, if it hit close to them then they're done for. Absolutely wasted. Don't even need to check for a pulse.

I first discovered this when I was out in my yard at night and got annoyed by some loud as hell crickets, so I did what any sane person would do with a can of chlorinated brake cleaner at their disposal, and blasted the loud ass nest in the sidewalk crack. Before it even hit the ground, 50 crickets came scrambling out having a seizure, and within seconds they were dead, and I had sweet sweet silence. Same fate for the spiders. Had a bunch of FAT ass spiders that made my skin crawl, absolute beasts. Just a couple satisfying trigger depressions of this and they were all curled up in little balls on the ground. But the best one yet, a whole ass rat. Two actually. That's right. A rat. I left my garage open at night and they snuck in, little bastards tried playing some funny games with me, dodging under the horde of dusty crap in here. Still, one screwed up and got cornered and I totally wet his ass with a 5 second blast. He scurried under a shelf and died right there. The second one was tricky, this wasn't his first rodeo, but it was surely his last. I got a hit in on him after a few minutes once I learned his route, and he ran away visibly distressed. Soon enough thougu, he joined his buddy. Shouldn't have been a dirty rat is all I gotta say.

I'll always have a can or two of this stuff on hand, nothing you can buy anywhere has as devastating an effect on pests as this does, and it doesn't even leave a stain!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Kills anything made of organic matter. Rodents, insects, you name it.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This is my 2nd time buying this item. The 1st one i purchased developed a leak after about a year. I am certain that leak was due to the amount of sheer abuse the poor bastard endured at the hands of myself and coworker alike. I work maintenance for a company that owns 11 different apartment complexes in town. There's only a couple of us that know how to repair air conditioners so from March through September I'm basically an Hvac tech. I used this manifold on roughly 5 to 15 units a day during these hotter months. Everything was fine until some jackass stole the case from the back of my truck one evening (i had been in a hurry to leave work that day so the actual manifold got to ride up front with me). After that the subject of this review no longer had a safe dwelling in which to accompany me on our adventures. So from then on it was constantly being smashed, bumped, knocked around and just flat out abused in the back of my truck. Im just saying it got real mfn acquainted with every ladder, tool box, refrigerator, water heater, brick, pipe and everything else that had ever been tossed in the back and taken for a ride. So, with that being said, just buy it and feel confident you got a quality item for a budget price.

The screw drivers that come with it are garbage so do yourself a favor and trash em immediately. The valve core removal tool is just ok. Everything else is great including the case but keep in mind that its not waterproof so in a heavy pour down water will make its way in. The gauges are accurate. I compared them with my friends high dollar yellow jacket set and a couple others over time. The gauges can also be field calibrated.. this is a big plus. The hoses are good quality. I did have a high side hose pop on me but who knows how much pressure that thing saw b4 being given up to the almighty. The unit I was examining developed a blockage somewhere and when I fired it up the last I saw b4 the line popped was the gauge reading 800+ psi and it appeared to be headed for the moon.

I wish I could give this item 10 stars. Its one of those rare finds thats got a price that makes me happy but performs like I spent a small fortune. Can't say enough good things. I'd put it up against any manifold in its class in every way and can almost guarantee it can't be beat. Throw in its unbelievably low cost and you might be so happy you quit your job.

P.s. Oh and just a little fyi... I took the leaky set home, removed the gauge heads from the manifold, cleaned the threads and Teflon taped them b4 I reinstalled them and guess what...? Yep! Good as new. What are you waiting for?
top_five_star_obscene.title: Why buy a more expensive set?

top_five_star_obscene.text:
LOUD! I am tired of morons that know they cannot mulitask, but still insist on talking or texting on their cell phone while driving, especially in TRAFFIC!!! MORONS!! Anyway, when you are your cell phone and you about shit your pants because you hear this, than you need to stop what you are doing and pay attention at your primary objective; driving a car! This is perfect loud, not to extreme and not too quiet like oem horns. This gets peoples attention and thats all i need it for. I don't abuse it, but i sure use it a lot. I have been through a few vehicles, only because of moron drivers not paying attention to what they are driving, like an automobile and crahsing into my car causing it to either be no driveable or insurance, my own insurance comapny screwing me over on the value of the vehicle. Now, with that said, these horns straighten drivers out to pay attention. Install it just right and it will work. Keep in mind 20 amp fuse. You can use the same horn configuration, just need to change the fuse from a 10a to a 20a and it will work.
*UPDATE* As of the begining of 2015, the horn has been inconsistent. It is to the point where i push the horn on the steering and all i hear is air blowing through the horns, no more loud noise. I didnt really notice, until awhile back i hit my horn, and i was not getting that same reaction like i have been. Now i know why. Unfortunately i have to buy a new one, no point of getting just the compressor, i would rather spend my money on something that will last.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Price did it for me, then hearing it made me smile!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This trash can beats the crap out of using a plastic bag in your in both function and style.

When I first opened this up I was quite surprised by it's quality. I guess I didn't really understand what I was getting. The material on this feels super tough and is pretty thick. It's not some thin flimsy nylon, it feels pretty high quality. The same goes for the inner liner. There's nothing I'm going to put in this that will come close to ripping or tearing the material. The liner is held in place by velcro and is easy to take out and put back in.

The lid can either be used or tucked down inside the bag area if you don't want to have to lift it all the time. The front pocket can be used for storing wet wipes and has a little opening for them or can just be regular storage. I'm using this in a 2002 Ford Explorer and I have it hanging off the back of the console and it's a great fit.

On top of it's pretty sweet function it looks nice. I always thought it was kinda ghetto to have some plastic bag hanging of somewhere in your car full of trash. This gives the whole thing a pretty fair look like you know it's trash but it's not trashy and helps you keep things clean. No more straw wrappers and receipts floating around. It's such a simple thing, being a car trash can, but I love it.

I received this product at a discounted price in exchange for my honest and unbiased review.
